text: The surgeon initiates laparoscopic access for the recurrent incisional hernia repair, utilizing minimally invasive techniques to visualize the hernia site.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply pressure from above on the contents of the hernia sac, gradually separating the adhesions and incarcerated omentum.
The surgeon performs the laparoscopic hernia repair using only two ports, visible in the abdominal area.
The surgical field shows the use of a harmonic scalpel and a single telescope for the laparoscopic hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins separating the tissue within the hernia sac, with gravity aiding in the downward movement of the tissue as it is released.
The surgeon uses fingers to gently pull the omental fat content back into the abdomen, gradually reducing the hernia sac.
The surgeon uses a finger to pull the omentum back inside the abdomen, completing the repositioning of the fatty tissue.
The surgeon continues to address adhesions around a large hernia, carefully separating tissue to manage the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses the left hand to press the omental content completely inside the abdomen, aiding in the reduction of the hernia.
The surgeon removes the hernia content, ensuring that the surrounding tissue remains intact and uninjured.

The surgeon addresses extensive omental adhesions in the lower abdomen, using a harmonic scalpel to separate them easily, ensuring the mesh can project six centimeters beyond the healthy margin of the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses the left hand to support the tissue from above while separating adhesions in the area of interest, ensuring clear access for the hernia repair.
The surgeon fixes the round ligament on the right side, securing it in place.
The surgeon clears adhesions from the peripheral margin, extending six centimeters from the hernia defect.
The surgeon prepares to apply a surgical mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it covers the cleared peripheral margin.
The surgeon displays the mesh, showing one side made of polyester and the other side of polyurethane, preparing it for placement in the surgical field.
The mesh is secured in place with Gore-Tex sutures applied at all four corners, ensuring stability and coverage over the hernia defect.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring the center of the mesh aligns with the center of the defect, as indicated by the pre-mapped sutures.
The surgeon positions the mesh two centimeters lateral to the corner of its margin, preparing for placement in the hernia repair procedure.
A small one millimeter stab wound is visible, created using an Alebu number knife.
The surgeon uses suture passers to individually pull the Gore-Tex sutures applied at the four corners of the mesh, ensuring secure placement over the hernia defect.
The surgeon demonstrates trans-facial fixation, securing the mesh in place over the hernia defect using sutures that pass through the facial tissue.
The surgeon uses a tool to stretch the mesh, ensuring it is positioned symmetrically across the hernia repair site.
The suture passer is maneuvered through the same skin incision but navigates through different fascial layers, including the rectus muscle.

The surgeon uses suture passers to pull each corner of the mesh separately, ensuring that at least one centimeter of tissue, including the rectus muscle and peritoneum, is positioned between the mesh and underlying structures.
The surgeon methodically fixes all corners of the mesh using a suture passer or a varice needle, ensuring secure attachment during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon creates a loop using a varice needle with vikril suture material, preparing to pull and secure the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon ties the suture outside the body, ensuring that the knot is positioned subcutaneously beneath the skin surface.
The surgeon pulls all the corners of the mesh to secure it in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon secures the last corner of the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon positions the last corner of the mesh towards Palmer's point, ensuring proper placement for the hernia repair.
The surgeon performs the final fixation at Palmer's point, securing the last corner of the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon completes the fixation of all four corners of the mesh in the hernia repair procedure, then carefully pulls the mesh taut and ties it securely.
The surgeon decreases the pressure and performs transfacial fixation of sutures at the corners, ensuring the mesh is bilaterally and symmetrically applied and stretched across the surgical site.

The surgeon uses titanium clips for trans-facial fixation of the mesh corners, ensuring secure attachment. The outer and inner crowning of the mesh is also performed using trans-facial fixation techniques.
The surgeon uses a protack device to apply titanium tackers around the mesh, securing it in place during the hernia repair procedure.
A grasper is used to support the mesh during the hernia repair procedure, ensuring stability as the mesh is positioned and secured.
The surgeon is currently fixing the outer sutures to secure the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon has nearly completed fixing sutures at all four corners of the hernia repair site.
The surgeon ensures that the blue-colored mesh faces towards the bowel during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon has completed fixing all the coordinates of the mesh, ensuring proper alignment and positioning for the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a protract device to begin fixing the surgical mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon secures the first fixation point, positioned furthest from their location, ensuring stability in the surgical field.

The surgeon positions the tacker at a two-centimeter distance from the previous fixation point, using the left hand to support the device. The tacker is aligned perpendicular to the abdominal wall, ensuring proper fixation by folding the wall to achieve a secure attachment.
The surgeon demonstrates the outer crowning of the mesh, ensuring it is properly positioned and secured within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a protract to fix the mesh from the center to the periphery, ensuring a smooth application without corrugation.
The surgeon positions the tacker to fire either lateral or medial to the inferior epigastric vessel, ensuring precise placement to avoid damaging the vessel.
The surgeon performs the inner crowning technique, manipulating the surgical mesh to ensure smooth placement without corrugation.
The surgeon observes the gradual decrease in pressure on the abdominal wall, facilitating the secure fixation of the hernia repair mesh.
The surgeon completes the fixation of the mesh around the hernia site, preparing to remove the insufflation gas from the abdominal cavity.
